Introduction to neural simulation-based inference in R

Source: vignettes/intro-to-sbi.Rmd
intro-to-sbi.Rmd

This is the first in a short series of tutorials on neural simulation-based inference (SBI) in R. It covers the basics: what problem SBI solves, the three ingredients every analysis needs, and what it means for a fit to be amortized. Later tutorials go deeper on density estimators, calibration, and applied case studies – this one stays small on purpose.

The problem

Ordinary Bayesian inference needs a likelihood, p(x | theta): a formula for how likely the data are, given the parameters. For a simulator – code that generates synthetic data by drawing random numbers internally – that formula often does not exist. The simulator can produce data all day long; it just cannot tell you the probability of any particular output. SBI sidesteps the likelihood entirely: it learns the posterior directly from (parameter, simulated-data) pairs.

A concrete example. The g-and-k distribution is a flexible model for skewed, heavy-tailed data – rainfall totals, incomes, financial returns – defined not by a density but by its quantile function: transform a standard normal draw z through

Q(z) = A + B * (1 + 0.8 * tanh(g * z / 2)) * (1 + z^2)^k * z

and the result is a draw from the distribution. A and B are location and scale; g and k control skew and tail weight. Simulating is a one-liner. But writing down the density means inverting Q(), which has no closed form – so the likelihood genuinely is not available, even though generating data is trivial. This is exactly the setting SBI targets, and it is why the distribution is a standard test case in the likelihood-free literature (Rayner & MacGillivray, 2002; Prangle, 2020).

We will treat g and k as known (2 and 0.5) and infer the location A and scale B from a small sample of observations.

The three ingredients

Every SBI problem needs a prior, a simulator, and an observation.

library(neuralsbi)   # note: masks base::sample -- see below
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'neuralsbi'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     sample

# (1) prior over the two parameters we want to infer
prior <- prior_uniform(low = c(0, 0.3), high = c(10, 2))

# (2) simulator: an (n x 2) matrix of parameters -> an (n x 10) matrix of data.
# Each row draws 10 i.i.d. observations from the g-and-k distribution at that
# row's (A, B), with g = 2 and k = 0.5 fixed.
rgk <- function(n, A, B, g = 2, k = 0.5, c = 0.8) {
  z <- rnorm(n)
  A + B * (1 + c * tanh(g * z / 2)) * (1 + z^2)^k * z
}
simulator <- function(theta) {
  t(apply(theta, 1, function(th) rgk(10, th[1], th[2])))
}

# (3) the observation: 10 measurements from a population with A = 3, B = 1
theta_true <- c(A = 3, B = 1)
x_obs <- simulator(rbind(theta_true))

neuralsbi masks base::sample() with an S3 generic so that sample(post, n) can dispatch on a fitted posterior; sample(1:10, 3) still works as usual, and sample_posterior() is a non-generic alias if the masking gets in the way in a script.

One more thing worth flagging up front, especially if you come from regression or Stan: SBI writes the observed data as x, the role your outcome variable y usually plays. Covariates, if you have any, are absorbed into the simulator. It takes a page or two to stop mentally substituting y.

Training an amortized posterior estimator

npe() (Neural Posterior Estimation) draws parameters from the prior, runs the simulator, and trains a density estimator – here a masked autoregressive flow (Papamakarios et al., 2017), the package default – to approximate p(theta | x) directly from those pairs. No likelihood function appears anywhere in this call.

# 5000 simulations for a 2-parameter posterior. If the fit below looks too
# wide or the calibration check is off, add simulations before reaching for a
# bigger network.
fit <- npe(prior, simulator, n_simulations = 5000, seed = 1)
fit
#> <nsbi_npe> Neural Posterior Estimation fit
#>   density estimator : maf
#>   parameters (dim)  : 2
#>   data (dim)        : 10
#>   simulations       : 5000
#>   best val loss     : 0.3304
#>   -> build a posterior with posterior(fit, x_obs = ...)

This fit is amortized: trained once, over the whole prior, it can be conditioned on any observation afterwards – no re-simulating, no retraining. That is the central practical payoff of NPE over MCMC-based approaches, which restart from scratch for every new data set.

Conditioning on the observation 

post   <- posterior(fit, x_obs = x_obs)
draws  <- sample(post, 5000)
colnames(draws) <- c("A", "B")

summary(draws)
#>   parameter     mean        sd      q2.5       q25      q50      q75    q97.5
#> 1         A 3.159921 0.3320365 2.5628756 2.9303117 3.136674 3.361319 3.893551
#> 2         B 1.070528 0.3944906 0.4336798 0.7381527 1.039410 1.392737 1.810212
pairplot(draws, truth = theta_true)

plot of chunk unnamed-chunk-4

The posterior concentrates around A = 3, B = 1 – the values that generated x_obs – with honest uncertainty from only 10 observations per data set.

What amortization buys you

Condition the same fit on a second, unrelated observation. There is no simulation and no training here – just a forward pass through the network already trained above.

theta_true2 <- c(A = 6, B = 0.6)
x_obs2      <- simulator(rbind(theta_true2))

draws2 <- sample(posterior(fit, x_obs = x_obs2), 5000)
pairplot(draws2, truth = theta_true2, labels = c("A", "B"))

plot of chunk unnamed-chunk-5

Reusing fit here is the point: an MCMC sampler would need to re-run its chains for x_obs2; the amortized posterior just looks up a new conditional density.

Checking the fit

A trained estimator always returns something – samples, a mean, credible intervals – whether or not the fit is any good. Checking calibration is part of the workflow, not an optional extra. Simulation-based calibration (SBC) does this without needing a known answer: draw theta from the prior, simulate x, and see where the true theta ranks among posterior draws given that x. Calibrated posteriors put the truth anywhere in that ranking with equal probability, so the ranks come out uniform.

res <- sbc(fit, simulator, n_sbc = 200, n_posterior_samples = 300, seed = 2)
res                # per-parameter uniformity p-values (large = calibrated)
#> <nsbi_sbc> 200 trials, 300 posterior samples each
#>   per-parameter uniformity p-values (large = calibrated):
#>     0.142  0.003

plot_sbc(res, param = 1)   # rank histogram for A: flat = calibrated

plot of chunk unnamed-chunk-6 

plot_coverage(res)         # empirical vs. nominal credible-interval coverage

plot of chunk unnamed-chunk-6

A’s ranks are close to flat and its coverage curve tracks the diagonal throughout. B is not quite there: its coverage dips modestly below the diagonal over the middle of the range – a nominal 65% interval covers closer to 55% – the signature of mild overconfidence. That is a realistic outcome for a first pass, not a failure: with only 10 observations per data set, scale is harder to pin down than location, and the fix is more simulations before reaching for a bigger network. vignette("diagnostics") covers the full set of checks, including what to do when a fit does not pass one.

Where to go next

This tutorial covered the minimum: prior, simulator, observation, an amortized fit, and one calibration check. It deliberately skipped almost everything else –

  • vignette("neuralsbi") walks the same workflow again with a linear-Gaussian model, where you can check the estimated posterior against an exact analytic answer.
  • vignette("density-estimators") compares the mixture-density-network, flow, and closed-form estimators, and when each is worth using.
  • vignette("diagnostics") covers the complete diagnostic toolkit: SBC, expected coverage, TARP, and posterior predictive checks.
  • vignette("sir-epidemic") is a full applied case study, including sequential inference when simulations are expensive and only one observation matters.

For the ideas behind the method: Cranmer, Brehmer & Louppe (2020), “The frontier of simulation-based inference”, is a short, readable review. Papamakarios & Murray (2016), “Fast ε-free inference of simulation models with Bayesian conditional density estimation”, introduced the approach NPE descends from. Talts et al. (2018), “Validating Bayesian inference algorithms with simulation-based calibration”, is the reference for the SBC check used above. Lueckmann et al. (2021), “Benchmarking simulation-based inference”, compares NPE against the other neural SBI algorithms this package does not implement (NLE, NRE) – useful context for where neuralsbi sits in the broader landscape. Rayner & MacGillivray (2002) and Prangle (2020) – the latter’s gk package – cover the g-and-k distribution itself.

On this page
